title: Noun gender and masculine animacy
summary: Predict the grammatical gender of many Czech nouns from their endings and distinguish masculine animate from masculine inanimate.
objectives: Identify masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns
Use common endings as practical clues

sections.heading: Natural and grammatical gender
sections.body: Words for people often follow natural gender, and -ka commonly creates a feminine occupational noun. Every Czech noun also has grammatical gender, including objects and ideas.

sections.heading: Practical ending clues
sections.body: A consonant ending usually suggests masculine. -a usually suggests feminine. -o and -í usually suggest neuter. Words ending in -e are often feminine, though some are neuter.

sections.heading: Masculine animacy
sections.body: Masculine nouns are divided into animate and inanimate. People and animals are normally animate; objects and plants are inanimate. This distinction matters for later case endings.

resources.title: Original lesson: Noun Gender
resources.note: Source for natural gender, ending clues, and masculine animacy.

exercises.prompt: What gender would you normally predict for kniha?

exercises.explanation: Učitel refers to a person and is masculine animate.

exercises.prompt: Type the common feminine form of profesor.
When uncertain about a common noun ending in -e, which prediction is often best?
exercises.explanation: Many common nouns ending in -e are feminine, although exceptions must be learned.
